options: Should be screened and treated to prevent pyelonephritis
s1.explain: Asymptomatic bacteriuria (2-10% of pregnancies) should be screened (urine culture at booking) and treated to prevent progression to pyelonephritis.

s1.q: The gold standard for diagnosing UTI in pregnancy is:
options: Urine culture with significant bacteriuria (≥10⁵ CFU/mL)
s1.explain: Urine culture with ≥10⁵ CFU/mL is the gold standard. Urine dipstick may be used for screening but has lower accuracy.

s1.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s1.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s1.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s1.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
Choice of investigation depends on the suspected condition.

s2.explain: Conservative management is reserved for mild, uncomplicated cases.

s2.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s2.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

options: Depends on the specific condition and gestational age
s2.explain: Treatment depends on the diagnosis, severity, gestational age, and patient factors.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
